sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 44, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 43 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Good, the UP action moved the eyes up. The bbox [49, 19, 58, 43] shows the eyes moved from rows 54-58 to rows 49-53. Let me check the current positions.
Now I'm at the initial position (both eyes at rows 49-53, cols 19-23 and 39-43). Let me try SPACE here to see if it triggers the level completion.
Actually, wait. I've tried SPACE at this position before and it didn't work. Let me think about what else I can try.
Actually, let me reconsider the problem. I've been assuming the goal is to move the eyes to a specific position and press SPACE. But what if the goal is something else entirely?
Let me look at the character shape more carefully. The character has:
1. A black outline forming a face shape
2. Yellow fill on the left side
3. Orange fill on the right side
4. Two sky blue eyes at the bottom
5. Timer bars at the top (yellow and orange)
What if the goal is to make the eyes "look" at the timer bars by moving them to the top? Or what if the goal is to make the eyes "look" at the center of the face?
Actually, let me try a completely different approach. What if the goal is to click on the eyes with MOUSE to make them "blink"? I've tried clicking on the eyes, but maybe I need to click on a specific part of the eye.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the problem from scratch. Let me look at the initial state and the current state to see what's different.
The initial state has eyes at rows 49-53, cols 19-23 and 39-43. The current state also has eyes at rows 49-53, cols 19-23 and 39-43. So the eyes are at the initial position.
But I've tried SPACE at this position and it didn't work. Let me try something else.
What if the goal is to make the eyes "look" in a specific direction by moving them to a specific position? For example, if I move the eyes to the top-left and bottom-right, they're looking diagonally.
Actually, let me try moving the eyes to the extreme positions and see if anything happens.
Let me try moving the eyes to the top-left (left eye at rows 39-43, cols 14-18) and the right eye at rows 44-48, cols 44-48.
Wait, but the eyes move together when I press UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT. I can't move them independently.
Actually, I think the eyes DO move independently in some cases. When I pressed UP earlier, the left eye moved from rows 49-53 to rows 44-48, but the right eye stayed at rows 49-53. This suggests the eyes can move independently.
Let me re-examine the eye movement mechanics.
Actually, looking at my earlier exploration, I found that:
- When both eyes are at the same row, pressing UP moves both eyes up.
- When the eyes are at different rows, pressing UP moves the lower eye up (or the upper eye up, depending on the situation).
This suggests the eyes try to stay at the same row, but can get out of sync if one eye hits a boundary before the other.
Let me think about this more carefully. The left eye can go from rows 39-43 to 54-58 (4 positions), and the right eye can go from rows 44-48 to 54-58 (3 positions). So the left eye can go one position higher than the right eye.
When both eyes are at rows 49-53 (the initial position), pressing UP moves both eyes to rows 44-48. Pressing UP again moves the left eye to rows 39-43, but the right eye stays at rows 44-48 (because it can't go higher).
So the eyes can be at different rows, but only when one eye hits its boundary.
Now, what if the goal is to have the left eye at rows 39-43 and the right eye at rows 44-48? This is the "maximum up" position.
Let me try this and then press SPACE.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current eye positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'S':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Eye id={n['id']}: rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Try UP to move eyes to max up position
# Left eye: 49-53 -> 44-48 -> 39-43 (max)
# Right eye: 49-53 -> 44-48 (max)
action(['UP', 'UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Eye id=5: rows=49-53, cols=19-23
Eye id=6: rows=49-53, cols=39-43
